posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I opened a wine the other day with an extra long DIAM 30 and it got me wondering about whether and how cork length can affect a wine, whether it’s the ageing curve or something else.

Anyone have any experience or insights into this?

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: I don’t know the answer, but I can guess what some of the responses are going to be.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I opened a wine the other day with an extra long DIAM 30 and it got me wondering about whether and how cork length can affect a wine, whether it’s the ageing curve or something else.

Anyone have any experience or insights into this?

If you listen to Levi’s interview with the DIAM CEO, he says DIAM 30 is longer mainly because of consumer perceptions. But DIAM is obviously a homogenous closure, whereas I suppose a longer cork in theory increases your chances of a tight seal.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
I opened a wine the other day with an extra long DIAM 30 and it got me wondering about whether and how cork length can affect a wine, whether it’s the ageing curve or something else.

Anyone have any experience or insights into this?

If you listen to Levi’s interview with the DIAM CEO, he says DIAM 30 is longer mainly because of consumer perceptions. But DIAM is obviously a homogenous closure, whereas I suppose a longer cork in theory increases your chances of a tight seal.

Thanks for pointing me to Levi’s podcast, I seem to have missed that episode, I’ll have to rectify that soon.

I’ve seen DIAM 30 in standard lengths as well. But the comment on Levi’s podcast on market perception certainly seems to ring true. A short while back there was a Berserker who recalled being at a DIAM symposium years ago with several high end Napa producers and many of them asking questions on the possibility of a DIAM 60 or an extra long version of the corks in order to portray a sense of premium in their wines.

The tech spec sheet on DIAM closures makes it sound like all lengths for a particular DIAM class have the same permeability. This makes me wonder whether DIAM is tweaking the corks specifications to get to the same permeability metrics across lengths or if the effect of the added length negligible in terms of permeability.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked: I would think that girth is more important.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: Since longer corks are more expensive, it was a prestige thing. The longest I’ve seen have been with Gaja. Beating out First Growth Bordeaux by a hair.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
I would think that girth is more important.

Yup, if you can’t properly fill the cavity, length is of no benefit. You are dead on!

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: Well, with natural corks I would think it lengthens the amount of time it takes for the cork to become fully saturated. With Diam, however — not so sure. Has anyone ever see a Diam that was appreciably saturated? I haven’t.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
I would think that girth is more important.

Yup, if you can’t properly fill the cavity, length is of no benefit. You are dead on!

I have heard the same
posts.display_username: Mikael B E K E L E

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Size isn’t important, its how big it is.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Well, with natural corks I would think it lengthens the amount of time it takes for the cork to become fully saturated. With Diam, however — not so sure. Has anyone ever see a Diam that was appreciably saturated? I haven’t.

DIAM is treated to prevent that.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Since longer corks are more expensive, it was a prestige thing. The longest I’ve seen have been with Gaja. Beating out First Growth Bordeaux by a hair.

I am always a bit disappointed by the corks used on top classified growth Bordeaux in the '80s and '90s and into the 2000s. I suspect one of the reasons Léoville-Las Cases evolves so slowly is that they actually buy high quality corks.
